Section.name: UNCLASSIFIED
Section.Content: Generic Service Catalogue [LEGACY - file not validated by Product Management] Service Catalogue Prepared for: Generic Service Catalogue [LEGACY - file not validated by Product Management] Prepared by: Lodewijk van der Sloot Date: [Publish Date] Cegeka reference: [Reference] Cegeka nv Corda 3, Kempische Steenweg 307 B-3500 Hasselt www.cegeka. com CONFIDENTIAL

Section.name: Style Guide
Section.Content: This document is a template that can be used to create a service catalogue for a customer. This customer may be a new customer or an existing customer undergoing contract renewal. The template includes all services in the English language. To create a service catalogue from this template, proceed as follows: After these steps you can make changes as necessary and review the service catalogue. Contents 1 Style Guide 2 2 Service description structure 12 2.1 Reading guide Service description 12 2.1.1 Standard services 12 2.1.2 Optional services 12 2.1.3 Out of scope 12 2.1.4 Conditions 12 2.1.5 Responsibilities 12 3 Integrated Cross-functional Services 13 3.1 Governance 13 3.1.1 Service management 13 3.1.2 Architecture management 14 3.1.3 Incident management 15 3.1.4 Request management 16 3.1.5 Problem management 16 3.1.6 Change management 17 3.1.7 Configuration management 17 3.1.8 Availability management 19 3.1.9 Capacity management 19 3.1.10 Responsibilities 21 3.2 Continuity management 24 3.2.1 Continuous improvement and innovation 24 3.2.2 Innovation meeting 25 3.3 Service desk 26 3.3.1 Introduction 26 3.3.2 Service description 26 3.4 Self Service function 27 3.4.1 Introduction 27 3.4.2 Service description 28 3.5 On-site Support 30 3.5.1 IMACD 30 3.5.2 Break & Fix 31 3.6 Security Operations Centre 32 3.6.1 Introduction 32 3.6.2 Security Operations Center services 32 3.6.3 Security Operations and Analytics Platform 33 3.6.4 Responsibilities 34 3.7 Security Expert 34 3.8 Disaster Recovery testing 35 3.8.1 Introduction 35 3.8.2 Service description 35 3.9 Monitoring 36 3.9.1 Introduction 36 3.9.2 Service description 37 3.9.3 Responsibilities 37 3.10 ISAE reporting 38 3.10.1 Service description 38 3.11 Software Asset Management 40 3.11.1 Introduction 40 3.11.2 Service description 40 3.11.3 Microsoft SPLA licenses 41 3.11.4 Responsibilities 42 4 Infrastructure Management 42 4.1 OS Management - Windows 42 4.1.1 Introduction 42 4.1.2 Service description 42 4.1.3 Responsibilities 45 4.2 OS Management – Linux 46 4.2.1 Introduction 46 4.2.2 Service description 46 4.2.3 Responsibilities 49 4.3 OS Management – AIX 50 4.3.1 Introduction 50 4.3.2 Service description 50 4.3.3 Responsibilities 51 4.4 OS Management – HP-UX (optional) 51 4.4.1 Introduction 51 4.4.2 Service description 51 4.5 OS Management – Power Hosting 53 4.5.1 Introduction 53 4.5.2 Service description 53 4.5.3 Responsibilities 55 4.6 Hypervisor Management 58 4.6.1 Introduction 58 4.6.2 Service description 58 4.6.3 Responsibilities 59 4.7 Technical application management 59 4.7.1 Introduction 59 4.7.2 Service description 60 4.8 Technical database management 64 4.8.1 Introduction 64 4.8.2 Service description 64 4.8.3 Responsibilities 64 5 Infrastructure Services 70 5.1 Compute IaaS 70 5.1.1 Introduction 70 5.1.2 Service description 71 5.1.3 Responsibilities 72 5.2 Storage SAN 72 5.2.1 Responsibilities 72 5.3 Storage NAS 73 5.3.1 Introduction 73 5.3.2 Service description 73 5.3.3 Responsibilities 74 5.4 Server connectivity Rack - SAN ports 74 5.4.1 Introduction 74 5.4.2 Responsibilities 75 5.5 SAN & storage device management 75 5.5.1 Introduction 75 5.5.2 Service description 75 5.5.3 Responsibility 77 5.6 Backup 78 5.6.1 Introduction 78 5.6.2 Service description 78 5.6.3 Responsibilities 79 5.7 Archiving 80 5.7.1 Introduction 80 5.7.2 Service description 80 5.8 Hardware Datacenter (Lease) 82 5.8.1 Introduction 82 5.8.2 Service description 82 5.8.3 Responsibilities 82 5.9 Software 83 5.9.1 Introduction 83 5.9.2 Service description 83 5.9.3 Responsibilities 83 5.10 FTP 84 5.10.1 Introduction 84 5.10.2 Service description 84 5.10.3 Responsibilities 84 5.11 SMS Gateway 85 5.11.1 Introduction 85 5.11.2 Service description 85 5.11.3 Responsibilities 85 6 Security Services 87 6.1 Firewall Services 87 6.1.1 Introduction 87 6.1.2 Service description 87 6.1.3 Responsibilities 88 6.2 Management dedicated platforms Firewall Security 89 6.4.1 Introduction 92 6.4.2 Service description 92 6.4.3 Responsibilities 93 6.5 Vulnerability scanning 93 6.5.1 Introduction 93 6.5.2 Service description 94 6.5.3 Responsibilities 94 6.6 Web Application Firewall 95 6.6.1 Introduction 95 6.6.2 Service description 96 6.7 Secure mail 96 6.7.1 Introduction 96 6.7.2 Service description 97 7 Network Services 97 7.1 Datacenter LAN Services 97 organization 117 8.2.4 Security best practices 118 8.2.5 Networking 118 8.2.6 Service Health 118 8.2.7 Billing 119 8.2.8 Monitoring 119 8.2.9 Integrations 119 8.2.10 Compliance 119 8.2.11 Cost management 120 8.2.12 Cost Optimization 120 8.3 Microsoft Azure Individual Components 120 8.3.1 Azure VMs 121 8.3.2 Storage accounts 121 8.3.3 Azure Bastion 121 8.3.4 AKS (Azure Kubernetes Service) 122 8.3.5 App service 122 8.3.6 Azure cache for Redis 122 8.3.7 Logic App 123 8.3.8 Azure Function 123 8.3.9 Azure Files 123 8.3.10 Azure Queues 124 8.3.11 Azure Tables 124 8.3.12 Azure SQL 124 8.3.13 Azure Database for MySQL 125 8.3.14 Azure Database for PostgreSQL 125 8.3.15 Azure Database for MariaDB 125 8.3.16 Azure Cosmos DB 126 8.3.17 Azure container registry 126 8.3.18 Azure Key Vault 126 8.3.19 Azure Site Recovery 126 8.3.20 Azure DNS 127 8.3.21 Azure S2S VPN Connection 127 8.3.22 Azure ExpressRoute (optional) 127 8.3.23 Azure CDN 128 8.3.24 Azure Load Balancer 128 8.3.25 Azure 9.14.2 Service description 156

Section.name: Reading guide Service description
Section.Content: Each Service description in this schedule contains the same paragraphs presenting a uniform template. In this chapter each standard paragraph is explained.

Section.name: Standard services
Section.Content: This section describes the Services that are to be delivered to <klant>. The Services include any services, activities and tasks that are of a nature and type that would ordinarily be performed as an inherent subtask by an entity (internal or external) performing Services similar to the Services and/or that are generally considered to be part of the Services.

Section.name: Optional services
Section.Content: This section describes the Services that can be optionally added to the Service delivered to <klant>.

Section.name: Out of scope
Section.Content: This section describes the explicitly defined out of scope services related to the Service.

Section.name: Conditions
Section.Content: This section describes the conditions that must be met in order for Cegeka to provide the Service.

Section.name: Responsibilities
Section.Content: ACTIVITY MATRIX <KLANT> CEGEKA Manage or always-on-vpn servers I R, A Provide access to users through directory services I R, A Service Part number(s) O.0560_0010_0010_A_TM.000 Image & OS Build Maintenance service - Fat client management (Included in)

Section.name: Service management
Section.Content: The objective of the service management process is to enable and support effective communication between Cegeka and <klant> regarding the provided services, helping to ensure the alignment between the expectations of <klant> and the service delivery of Cegeka. Cegeka aims to provide optimal support of <klant>’s IT processes, by implementing service management. A service delivery manager is appointed for <klant>. During the service intake process of <klant>, the service delivery manager customizes the Cegeka generic IT service delivery processes and documents this in the service delivery plan. The processes are based on ITIL. The service delivery plan is reviewed at least annually and is updated whenever changes are made to the service. This service delivery plan will be created during the setup service organization stream of the transition. Cegeka manages and guards the agreed service levels. The service delivery manager consolidates the metrics, defined in the SLA, in the service status report and reports if the agreed-upon service level targets were met or not. The service status report is used to discuss the results with <klant> during the service review meeting. To meet the objectives of the Service Management process, the Service Delivery Manager operates on all 3 layers within the governance model. On an operational level this includes the following activities and responsibilities: On a tactical level this includes the following activities and responsibilities: On a strategic level this includes the following activities and responsibilities Service Part number(s) 0.0010_0010_0010_M_TM.000 Service Manager

Section.name: Architecture management
Section.Content: Lead Architect The Cegeka Lead Architect is responsible for the architectural design, control and optimization of the contractual services. This is reflected in his accountability for the availability and quality of the technical plans, e.g. monitoring, availability, back-up and disaster recovery plans. He/she will furthermore support the Service Delivery Manager and Account Manager in creating improvement proposals from a technological perspective based upon the business needs of the customer. He will support the Pre-Sales and Account Manager in designing the solution and calculating the cost when proposing new services to Customer. The Lead Architect: Service Part number(s) 0.0010_0040_0010_M_TM.000 Lead Architect The Cegeka Technical Architect is responsible for the technical architecture, operations and optimization of the contractual services. This is reflected in e.g. preparing a monitoring, availability, back-up and disaster recovery plan. Configuration Management is also operated by the Technical Architect. He/she will furthermore support the Service Delivery Manager and Account Manager in creating proposals from a technological perspective. He will support the Pre-Sales and Account Manager in calculating the cost when proposing new services to Customer but will also provide cost estimates and impact analyses for RFC’s. The Technical Architect: Service Part number(s) 0.0010_0040_0020_M_TM.000 Technical Architect

Section.name: Incident management
Section.Content: The primary goal of the incident management process is to restore normal service operation as quickly as possible when incidents occur and minimize the adverse impact on <klant>’s IT and/or business operations, thus ensuring that the agreed levels of service quality and availability are maintained. 'Normal service operation' is defined here as service operation as defined in the Service Levels. Cegeka’s Incident Management responsibilities span the services delivered by Cegeka and include: Service Part number(s) 0.0010_0010_0040_M_TM.000 Incident Manager 0.0010_0010_0010_M_TM.000 Service Manager (Act in smaller teams also as Incident Manager)

Section.name: Request management
Section.Content: Cegeka is responsible for tracking, managing, processing and fulfilling <klant> appropriately approved requests in accordance with the operational documentation. These requests are standard and non-standard requests for provided new, contracted Services and/or managed infrastructure. A request will always be raised with priority P4. Cegeka’s request management responsibilities include: Service Part number(s) 0.0010_0010_0020_M_TM.000 Change Manager 0.0010_0010_0010_M_TM.000 Service Manager (Acts in smaller teams also as Change Manager)

Section.name: Problem management
Section.Content: The problem Management process is responsible for managing the lifecycle of all problems related to the Services. The primary objectives of Problem Management are to avoid incidents from happening, to eliminate recurring incidents and to minimize the impact of those incidents that cannot be prevented. Cegeka Problem Management responsibilities include: Service Part number(s) 0.0010_0010_0030_M_TM.000 Problem Manager 0.0010_0010_0010_M_TM.000 Service Manager (Act in smaller teams also as Problem Manager)

Section.name: Change management
Section.Content: Change Management is the process responsible for managing lifecycle of all Changes. The primary objective of Change Management is to standardize the process of introducing and executing changes to the managed infrastructure. These changes may arise reactively in response to problems or externally imposed requirements (e.g. legislative changes), or proactively from seeking improved efficiency and effectiveness or to enable or reflect business, programs, projects or service improvement initiatives. Cegeka will use its Change Management Tracking system to track changes relating to the scope of this contract. Standard Changes are defined as standard, non-complex changes in the datacenter or managed infrastructure in scope, with pre-defined impact assessments and price, executed according to pre-defined procedures. Moreover, Standard Changes must have the following attributes: Changes that are handled through the Change Management process are characterized by any of following: The tasks to carry out are complex because of interdependencies and need close co-operation with <klant> in order to be implemented (e.g. in case of Managed infrastructure adaptations in an application release weekend). The change management process does not handle <klant>’s requests (a.k.a. nonstandard service requests) for new Services/Products/Solutions that have been categorized as Cegeka Projects by <klant>. These will follow the project management agreement. Service Part number(s) 0.0010_0010_0020_M_TM.000 Change Manager 0.0010_0010_0010_M_TM.000 Service Manager (Act in smaller teams also as Change Manager)

Section.name: Configuration management
Section.Content: The configuration management process is responsible for creating and maintaining a library of information and documentation for any existing, new, enhanced or modified configuration item of the IT Infrastructure within scope of the Services. Configuration management is a joined effort of Cegeka and <klant>. Each party needs to supply up-to-date, correct and complete information to be able to create and maintain the configuration management system (CMS). Cegeka will use its configuration management system to track configuration items (CI’s) relating to the scope of this contract. The following table shows if CI types are in the CMDB of <klant> or at Cegeka. Each company is responsible for keeping its respective CI’s accurate and up-to-date. CI types that are in multiple CMDB’s are managed by both <klant> and Cegeka in their own CMDB tool. CI Type <klant> Cegeka Software (Business Application) X Laptops (end-user devices) X Printers X Servers (physical and virtual) X Network components (switches, routers, AP’s, firewalls, etc.) X X People (contacts) X X <klant> will not have real-time insight in the Cegeka CMDB. A report containing an export of the content of the CMDB can be delivered. This is an automated process. Cegeka’s configuration management responsibilities include: Service Part number(s) 0.0010_0040_0020_M_TM.000 Technical Architect

Section.name: Availability management
Section.Content: The goal of Availability Management is to optimize availability of the managed infrastructure including the IT Infrastructure, services and supporting organization, to deliver a cost effective and sustained level of availability that enables the business to satisfy its business objectives. Cegeka’s Availability Management process is embedded via the same monitoring process as the one for Capacity Management. Cegeka ensures to have ample resources available for the future by using monitoring tools to detect trends (what we know) and capture required growth from <klant> orders and quotations and our own growth predictions, etc. (what we expect). This service applies to all the Shared Platform functionality, including storage performance and capacity, virtual computing, networking and also environmental like power, cooling, protective measures, data room space, etc. The lead architect is responsible for defining the monitoring plan. Infrastructure availability reports will be attached to the service review report. Service Part number(s) 0.0010_0010_0010_M_TM.000 Service Manager

Section.name: Capacity management
Section.Content: Capacity management is part of Cegeka’s continuous service delivery and shall ensure that IT resources are sized right to meet current and future business requirements in a cost-effective manner. It covers multiple aspects from capacity analysis to capacity planning. Capacity analysis is concerned with: Component capacity management Component capacity management is embedded in Cegeka’s monitoring process. The Cegeka monitoring process provides various options for automated monitoring and alerting. If there are spikes in, for example, processing power at a particular time of the day, it shall analyse what is happening at that time and make Changes to maximize the existing IT infrastructure, for example, move a batch cycle to a quieter period. It is implemented as follows: processing which deviates above installed thresholds, will generate an alert, which is handled via the incident management process. Recurring alerts will be handled through the problem management process for the alert analysis and for the solution, which is implemented via the Change management. This component capacity management is imbedded in the daily operational processes for <klant>. The monitoring team implements the monitoring and alerting scheme. Alerts generate an incident, which is handled through the Incident Management process. Monitoring data is accessible and available for review (performance management). The monitoring data is retained for at least one year. During service delivery, the technical architect keeps the monitoring plan up-to-date and the service delivery manager reviews the monitoring plan at least once a year. Specific thresholds, work instructions and process interfaces will be agreed and elaborated on during Transition. Resource consumption is measured on infrastructure level and part of the monthly service reporting. Following capacity documents are available: Capacity trending Another aspect of capacity management is capacity trending. As the usage of the IT infrastructure and Services Change and functionality evolves, resource usage can Change and lead to capacity shortage if not managed. Capacity trending is based on historical data, enriched with impact from known Change (known to Cegeka) and extrapolated to the near future. For <klant>, capacity trending shall be analysed and formally reported by Cegeka. SCOPE Non-exhaustive list: FREQUENCY As agreed in the service delivery plan. MEETING Reported in Architecture Review Meeting CONTENT The report contains: The capacity reports will be used by Cegeka for pro-active maintenance purposes. The pro-active maintenance process is part of the problem management process. The Cegeka technical architect will analyse the capacity management reports in combination with monitoring trend reports. Detailed problem analysis will be performed by the Cegeka engineering team and with following deliverables as output: Capacity planning The demand governance of the architecture board will capture, analyse and consolidate upcoming Changes, and will trigger high-level capacity plans in an early stage. This, on top of the capacity trending in place, will give a proactive, upfront, consolidated plan for adding capacity in anticipation of an increase in demand. Service Part number(s) 0.0010_0040_0010_M_TM.000 Lead Architect 0.0010_0040_0020_M_TM.000 Technical Architect

Section.name: Continuity management
Section.Content: Cegeka’s Lead Architect is at the centre of the process, assisted by the Service Delivery Manager, Account Manager, Security Expert and Technical Architect(s) and gathering input from both <klant> and Cegeka’s architects. Their activities are based on two building blocks: An innovation meeting is organized, and execution and follow-up of this workshop is part of our ISAE 3000 SOC 2 audit.

Section.name: Continuous improvement and innovation
Section.Content: Continuous innovation always follows the same standard process approach, regardless of the level of focus (technical, process or governance): Phase 1: Establish baseline – the baseline relies upon the corner stones of operations – architecture, service performance and cost – which are kept up to date as part of the daily operations. Phase 2: Identify opportunities – opportunities are detected throughout operations. While almost anyone in the service organisation and <klant>’s organisation can identify opportunities, it is the responsibility of our Lead Architect to keep track and prioritise them. During half-yearly workshops prepared by the Lead Architect, the topics are prepared with a clear vision in mind (see “Continuous innovation workshop”). Phase 3: Develop capture strategy – based on the list of identified and agreed opportunities a strategy is developed to ensure how the opportunities can be “captured” and integrated in the organisation’s planning via an implementation roadmap. Smaller opportunities with a short-term return can already be initiated as an RFC via the existing change management process. Phase 4 : Realise benefits – the roadmap provides the plan for implementation and must contain the business case on which the priority decisions have been made. Phase 4 contains the actual implementation of the opportunity at whatever level it may be – technical, procedure/process level, governance – and looks back at the initial business case(s) to ensure the benefits are realised. Safeguarding the actual quality of the services the continuous improvement methodology is applied to a number of architecture deliverables, which have interfaces with one another and provide input to the continuous improvement roadmap. An example of the deliverables plotted over time, is given below: Deliverables that are continuously maintained: The implementation of these deliverables and the yearly update is part of our ISAE 3000 SOC2 audit.

Section.name: Innovation meeting
Section.Content: The continuous innovation workshops are key in our process. About one month before the actual workshop, Cegeka will start preparing it with a core team of Service Delivery Manager, Account Manager and Technical Architects coordinated by Cegeka’s Lead Architect. To ensure consistency and synergy Cegeka’s process owner of continuous innovation as well as the Lead Architects’ manager are involved. This core team puts together a draft agenda using a subset of <klant>’s business drivers acting as central theme and a high-level roadmap showing both ongoing initiatives and new opportunities. The existing and continuously managed architecture overview is referenced wherever needed. The draft agenda is also discussed with <klant> prior to the meeting to ensure expectations are aligned. For each continuous innovation topic, a proposal is prepared containing: These proposals are presented and discussed during the workshop itself to reach a consensus on how to proceed (or not) with the proposed initiatives. The outcome can range from approval over further discussion or in-depth workshops to postponement or cancellation. As part of the workshop, Cegeka’s solution portfolio and its implementation roadmap is presented in support of the proposed opportunities and to share our vision on the near future.

Section.name: Introduction
Section.Content: The print service delivers the management and operation for the printer queues of all supported and contracted network printers, multi-functionals and plotters. The objective of this Service is to manage the local printing infrastructure from a technical perspective to support printing functionality for authorized users. This Service includes the operational monitoring and administration for the print management service infrastructure.

Section.name: Service description
Section.Content: Standard services Cegeka shall be responsible in providing the following services: Optional services Out of scope The provisioning of printers, multifunctional devices and associated supplies are not included in this Service. Conditions Data management policies are provided by the customer ↑ On request we can deliver this information ↑ Exchange Online Protection only ↑ requires permissions to the external DNS portal ↑ requires permissions to the external DNS portal ↑ Management of SharePoint does not include content management or custom components ↑ requires Active Directory management ↑ requires Active Directory management ↑

Section.name: On-site Support
Section.Content: As part of Cegeka’s workplace offering, we offer IMACD and Break & Fix onsite services on a global scale. Below a description of these services can be found.

Section.name: IMACD
Section.Content: IMACD’ stands for Installs, Moves, Adds, Changes and Disposal – the five primary needs of IT infrastructure. With more and more companies gearing up to meet the fast-paced challenges of the business world, the need for IMACD services is growing. The Cegeka IMACD services offer long and short-term IMACD solutions for a wide range of organizational needs. Our services complement our workplace outsourcing services by providing skilled professionals, with extensive knowledge, unique skill sets and the ability to handle peak workloads; thus allowing Customers to concentrate on core business areas. We offer immediate access to our trained staff and the tools to support both your non-routine and unique needs, to ensure business continuity. We handle IMACD activity of any size or scope and provide IMACD services in and out of normal working hours, to give your business the most flexibility. The following list provides you a list of IMACD activities: ACTIVITY TYPE DESCRIPTION DEVICE Install Workplace staging Workplace install Peripheral install Move Move workplace or peripheral Add Installation of additional peripherals Change Smart hands Disposal Removal of workplace equipment

Section.name: Break & Fix
Section.Content: The Cegeka Break & Fix services (onsite service) are available in two standard flavours: basic and premium. Cegeka basic onsite services will establish the necessary service management structures with local staff to be able to act according to agreed SLAs in case of hardware failure. In this case hardware for spare parts is owned by the Customer’s organization and the availability on site is also ensured by the Customer. To assist in this preparation, Cegeka of course is able to plan and organize all necessary tasks together with the Customer to prepare the environment for SLA-based onsite operation (optional). Cegeka premium onsite services will establish the necessary service management structures with local staff including the necessary spare part handling to be able to act according to agreed SLAs. In this case hardware for spare parts is owned by the Customer’s organization but the local availability as well as care pack repair in case of defects and refill of spare stocks, tracking & reporting is managed by Cegeka as part of the service. The standard SLA is ‘end of next business day’ and will ensure that incidents are fixed by the end of the next local business day at the latest. It is possible to agree on specific SLAs, spare policies and local tasks as part of Cegeka’s individual onsite service.

Section.name: Security Operations Center services
Section.Content: The SOC services are delivered by the Cegeka SOC and include the operational day-to-day activities needed to detect and respond to security incidents. The aim of the SOC is to: Security incident management Cegeka monitors the alarms generated by the SOAP and uses this platform for “hunting” undiscovered alerts. Next to security incident discovery, the aim of hunting is to detect new opportunities for the production or modification of scenarios. The output of the SOAP includes system outputs, trend analysis, anomalies and Cegeka threat intelligence. After analysis, the output is declared as a security incident, classified as Priority 1, 2, 3 or 4 and communicated to the appropriate resolution group according to an agreed communication plan. Depending on the priority of the incident, this can be by email or phone or any other communication channel and will be based on (an) existing escalation process(es) as much as possible. Cegeka provides a recommended remediation, and assists the resolution team in charge with performing root cause and impact analysis and managing the status of the security incident ticket. The problem management process is used to investigate the lessons learned and propose additional measures. The change management process is used to implement the additional measures. Service Part number(s) 0.0040_0020_0040_M_TM.000 Siem governance (monthly)

Section.name: Security Operations and Analytics Platform
Section.Content: The SOAP gives Cegeka SOC the tool to quickly detect and respond to internal and external attacks. The platform simplifies threat management while minimizing risk and safeguarding <klant> business. The SOAP is built on the Cegeka Operational Intelligence Platform (SOIP). Running from the Cegeka datacenters located in Hasselt (Belgium) and/or Geleen (Netherlands), it provides full end-to-end monitoring of the <klant> private- and public clouds. The SOAP provides insight into machine data generated from security technologies such as network, endpoint, access, malware, vulnerability and identity information. Reporting and dashboards The SOAP provides a library of security posture dashboards. This way Cegeka has a view on security events by location, host, source type, asset groupings and geography. KPIs provide trending and monitoring of your security posture. The SOAP comes with predefined key indicators that identify key security metrics for the security domains in scope. Scheduled reports deliver key indicators by email. Cegeka provides <klant> a secured webportal only accessible over the internal <klant> network showing: Remarks: Cegeka provides <klant> below standard reports: Remarks: Service Part number(s) O.0420_0010_0010_A_S.000 Siem SIEM subscription O.0420_0010_0020_A_S.000 Siem SIEM data log consumption O.0420_0010_0030_A_S.000 Siem Back-end systems in scope O.0420_0010_0040_A_S.000 Siem Workstation systems in scope

Section.name: Security Expert
Section.Content: The security expert profile is used to ensure that the delivered Services are in line with the agreed Standards and as defined in the security agreement. As such, the security expert can be used for compliance, audit, privacy, security reporting, awareness and advisory activities on any security and compliance matter during the Term of the Agreement. The Security expert liaises between the <klant> security & compliance actors and the Cegeka core and technical teams. The security expert can: Service Part number(s)

Section.name: Monitoring
Section.Content: An Azure environment can be monitored by different tools but Cegeka by default uses Azure Monitor due to its native and deep integration with other Azure services. Azure monitor provide an extensive set of functionalities allowing to gain visibility on the health and historical behaviour of Azure resources, including: The activities included in this service being influenced by the amount and diversity of Azure resources, its price is included inside individual components. This service includes the integration into the Cegeka Monitoring platform. This is needed to make sure alerts are triggered and the operational support people are informed immediately.

Section.name: ISAE reporting
Section.Content: Cegeka has built a control framework based upon its Quality Management System (QMS) and its Information Security Management System (ISMS). On a yearly basis, an independent service auditor examines Cegeka’s control framework, which is also applicable to <klant>, and expresses an opinion on the suitability of the design and the operating effectiveness of the controls to achieve Cegeka’s control objectives. The audit and the report are completed in accordance with ISAE 3000. Such a report is typically an auditor-to-auditor report: With regards to information security, Cegeka has extended his Quality Management System, Information Security Management System and control framework with best practices from ISO 27002. The audit of this extended control framework resulted in an ISAE 3000 report, also called an 'ISO 27002 in control statement'.

Section.name: Microsoft SPLA licenses
Section.Content: Cegeka can provide Microsoft licensing to <klant>, based on SPLA. This model is a pay per use on a monthly basis model (Server OS licences (Windows or RHEL) are standard offered for the systems on the infrastructure service Compute IaaS). Where possible according to Microsoft license policies, Cegeka can provide SPLA licenses to <klant>. A formal order form has to be signed to order or remove certain licenses. SPLA licenses are calculated on a monthly basis. Cegeka reports and pays the number of agreed SPLA licenses towards Microsoft and invoices <klant> for these licenses. Licenses can be adjusted monthly with a 7 days prior notification (formal order or change of the order). License compliancy is always a responsibility of <klant>. Using SPLA licenses via Cegeka does not give license compliancy guarantees for <klant> at any moment in time. It can be a part of the complete Microsoft licensing package, which <klant> has for compliancy. The Cegeka invoices may serve as compliance proof towards Microsoft but only for the SPLA licenses. The following conditions apply:

Section.name: Responsibility
Section.Content: ACTIVITY MATRIX <klant> CEGEKA Firewall Security Management Technical platform management I R, A Change management C R, A Scheduling and taking backups of the configuration files from the device to a Cegeka central system C ,I R, A Managing the log file generation and storing the log files of the device provided by the customer on a system of STAID for at least 6 months (maximum 1GB per month); C, I R, A Account and access management by central account repository C,I R, A Creating, maintain and test device recovery plans C, I R, A Executing standard requests I R, A Executing non-standard requests I R, A On-site interventions outside Cegeka data center A, I R, I Service Part number(s) O.0390_0020_0020_A_S.000 Management dedicated platforms -Firewall Security

Section.name: Vulnerability scanning
Section.Content: This chapter is considered to be a draft outline and will be discussed in a workshop if <klant> decides to implement this solution.

Section.name: 7.3.2 Service description
Section.Content: Standard services Cegeka shall be responsible in providing the following Services: Optional services Two-factor authentication Out of scope N/A Conditions This Service does not require any explicit conditions to be met. Part Number O.0480_0010_0030_A_S.000 VPN Monthly cost for client-to-site VPN

Section.name: RBAC
Section.Content: RBAC (Role Based Access Control) is the name given to the process regulating the permissions granted to users with regards to Azure resources. These permissions define the actions that a user is allowed to perform (ex: read only, configuration modification, switch on/off,…) on Azure resources. These permissions are resource specific so a user can have reader rights on a set of resource, owner rights on other resources and no right on the rest. The RBAC management activity consists in continuously reviewing the state of these permissions, providing recommendations and modifying these permissions upon request.

Section.name: Governance and organization
Section.Content: Over time, as consumption scales, Azure resources tend to proliferate and could become hardly manageable without appropriate governance and organization. The Cegeka resource/subscription management service ensures that Azure resources remain organized based on a pre-defined structure, for instance based on use case, delegation model or customer internal organization. This service also simplifies other daily operations by keeping resources conform with a predefined tagging and naming convention. Finally it ensures further scalability by checking that sufficient room remains compared to Azure limits and initiating pro-active actions when limits are getting close.

Section.name: Security best practices
Section.Content: The base service includes the management of globally applicable security mechanisms based on global tools (individual security components or configurations are covered separately). Essentially the service focusses on managing security tools like Microsoft Defender for Cloud, Azure DDoS and Azure advisor as well as ensuring that Cegeka and Microsoft security best practices are applied.

Section.name: Networking
Section.Content: The VNet service includes the management of Azure networking resources providing basic connectivity inside Azure. Components providing advanced functionalities (LB, Application gateway,…) or connectivity outside of Azure are covered separately.

Section.name: Service Health
Section.Content: Service health provides a personalized view of the health of the Azure services and regions you're using. This is the best place to look for service impacting communications about outages, planned maintenance activities, and other health advisories because the authenticated Service Health experience knows which services and resources you currently use. The management and resolution of backbone level incidents is included in the base service package. Backbone incidents include incidents not related to an individual resource like zonal/regional unavailability.

Section.name: Billing
Section.Content: Cegeka provides billing services for the Azure consumption of CSP customers. This service is included inside the base service package. This doesn’t mean that the base service price includes Azure charges but that these Azure charges can be billed by Cegeka, jointly with other Cegeka services.

Section.name: Integrations
Section.Content: This service includes the integration into the Cegeka support platforms. This is needed to support and manage the environments with our engineers and architects, in an efficient and secure way. You also get access to our incident management portal to allow incident creation and follow-up on support tickets.

Section.name: Compliance
Section.Content: Azure offers a lot of new possibilities. The breed of possibilities may introduce a challenge to keep resources compliant without reducing choice freedom. Cegeka helps solving this issue by decoupling compliancy from design. The compliancy service consists in managing a predefined set of compliancy rules and to leverage Azure policy to continuously monitor the compliancy state of deployed resources with the predefined rules.

Section.name: Cost management
Section.Content: As Azure environments scale, it remains important to keep consumption under control, traceable and manageable. Azure environments are typically used to host applications from different company departments and cost centers. Cegeka cost management leverages different Azure tools to help customers understand their consumption, keep it under desired limits and enable cost allocation, chargeback or showback strategies.

Section.name: Cost Optimization
Section.Content: Azure offers multiple ways to consume its resources and the charges also generally depend from the use duration or sizes of selected resources. Consequently, it is important to keep azure consumption aligned with real needs. The cost optimization service of Cegeka is meant to optimize the consumption by continuously scanning deployed resources, providing optimization recommendations and reduce charges.

Section.name: Microsoft Azure Individual Components
Section.Content: Besides the base service Cegeka delivers several other services for Microsoft Azure. Typically these services matches the services delivered by Microsoft. Cegeka performs additional services in this regard.

Section.name: Azure VMs
Section.Content: Azure virtual machines like traditional VM’s provide full control on inner content (operating system, application & configuration) to administrators. However, while they do not compare with platform features built in other PaaS offerings, Azure VM’s offer more features than traditional VM’s and Cegeka can manage these features making Azure VM’s more cloud native. A prerequisite for this add on is that an existing Disaster Recovery Plan is in place. Some features are covered by others services

Section.name: Azure Bastion
Section.Content: Azure Bastion provides secure and seamless RDP/SSH connectivity to all virtual machines in a VNet, directly in the Azure portal over TLS. With Azure Bastion, virtual machines do not need a public IP address and their RDP/SSH ports is not exposed to the outside world. Azure Bastion provides access to the virtual machine directly from the Azure portal without additional client, agent, or piece of software.

Section.name: AKS (Azure Kubernetes Service)
Section.Content: While containers exist since a long time, they gained more substantial adoption over the last years. During this period of wider adoption, different container orchestration platforms co-existed for a while but Kubernetes reached the highest consensus and is now considered as the standard container orchestration platform, among other thanks to its ability to preserve container portability across on-premises and multi-cloud environments (in contrast to other proprietary solutions).

Section.name: App service
Section.Content: Azure App service allows users to easily deploy, build and scale web apps created with .NET, .NET Core, Node.JS, Java, PHP, Ruby or Python, in containers or running on any operating system.

Section.name: Azure cache for Redis
Section.Content: Azure Cache for Redis is a fully managed, in-memory cache that enables high-performance and scalable architectures. Use it to create cloud or hybrid deployments that handle millions of requests per second at sub-millisecond latency – all with the configuration, security and availability benefits of a managed service. While most databases store data on slower, disk-based storage, Azure Cache for Redis stores data in memory. Since memory is significantly faster than disk storage, data can be written and retrieved much more quickly. Adding Azure Cache for Redis can increase data throughput by over 800 percent while increasing latency performance by over 1,000 percent at a cost-effective price. It’s a great way to efficiently scale applications and improve user experience without the expense of rearchitecting your underlying database.

Section.name: Logic App
Section.Content: Like Azure Function, Logic App also helps developers to build modern application architecture. Logic App provides an orchestration platform to build automated workflows. Logic App provides extensive built-in connectors with third-party (Salesforce, SAP, Oracle, Twitter,…) or Microsoft (O365, Dynamics, BizTalk, Azure,…) services for simple enterprise integration services. It is not only serverless but also requires no code at all. Cegeka can help developers to adopt this kind of patterns by offloading non application-level activities, allowing developers to focus on the business and application logic.

Section.name: Azure Function
Section.Content: Azure Function allows modern application architectures to be created, including patterns like event-based processing, microservices, LowCode (leveraging built-in features & integration), serverless, auto-scaling, cloud native (platform integration)… Cegeka can help developers to adopt this kind of patterns by offloading them from non application-level activities, allowing them to focus on the business and application logic.

Section.name: Azure Files
Section.Content: Azure files allows organizations to host SMB or NFS file shares in the cloud without managing server infrastructure.

Section.name: Azure Queues
Section.Content: Azure Queue Storage provides storage for large numbers of messages. Messages can be accessed from anywhere in the world via authenticated calls using HTTP or HTTPS and queues can scale very high (millions of messages). Messaging services are used to decouple applications, providing scalability based on asynchronous processing. Azure Queues is a simple messaging service providing very high scalability and complementing more advanced messaging solutions of Azure like Event Grid & Service Bus.

Section.name: Azure Tables
Section.Content: Azure Table storage provides structured NoSQL data storage, providing a key/value store with a schema-less design. Because Table storage is schema-less, it's easy to adapt data structure as application needs evolve. While Azure Table provides a simple and cost efficient NoSQL table storage, it could be at term be replaced by Azure Cosmos DB Table API.

Section.name: Azure SQL
Section.Content: Azure SQL provides a PaaS platform to host SQL workloads. It provides different form factors with different customization and compliancy levels with on-premises workloads, including SQL managed instances, SQL databases & elastic pools. The service described below focusses on managing the functionalities provided by Azure SQL allowing to host SQL workloads, it does not include pure database management.

Section.name: Azure Database for MySQL
Section.Content: Azure Database for MySQL provides a PaaS platform to host MySQL workloads. It provides multiple tiers and consumption model like reservations or PAYG. The service described below focusses on managing the functionalities provided by Azure to support and integrate Azure Database for MySQL and to keep it safe and secure, it does not include pure data management tasks.

Section.name: Azure Database for PostgreSQL
Section.Content: Azure Database for PostgreSQL provides a PaaS platform to host PostgreSQL workloads. It provides multiple tiers and consumption model like reservations or PAYG. The service described below focusses on managing the functionalities provided by Azure to support and integrate Azure Database for PostgreSQL and to keep it safe and secure, it does not include pure data management tasks.

Section.name: Azure Database for MariaDB
Section.Content: Azure Database for MariaDB provides a PaaS platform to host MariaDB workloads. It provides multiple tiers and consumption model like reservations or PAYG. The service described below focusses on managing the functionalities provided by Azure to support and integrate Azure Database for MariaDB and to keep it safe and secure, it does not include pure data management tasks.

Section.name: Azure container registry
Section.Content: Azure container registry provides storage and management for container images. It integrates closely with different other Azure services like AKS, App Service and Azure pipelines but can also be used with third party products like Docker Swarm, DC/OS,…

Section.name: Azure Key Vault
Section.Content: Azure Key Vault provides a central location to securely store keys, passwords, certificates, and other secrets, eliminating unsafe practices like storing credentials inside application code, blob storage or developer files,… Azure Key Vault features multiple layers of redundancy to make sure that keys and secrets remain available to application even if individual components of the service fail.

Section.name: Azure Site Recovery
Section.Content: Azure Site Recovery (ASR) provides disaster recovery service, typically between two Azure regions but it can also recover on-premises VMs to Azure or to another on-premises datacenter. ASR restores VMs based on recovery points which can be of two types: crash-consistent and app-consistent recovery points. Crash-consistent recovery points only include disk data while app-consistent ones also include memory data flushed by the application at snapshot time. If desired, ASR can also automatically deploy the target environment (subscription, resource group, vnet, storage account, replica managed disks, availability zones & availability sets). Target VMs are only created when a failover is initiated (avoiding useless charges). By default, the failover occurs manually (single click/cli command) but could be scripted if trigger conditions can be clearly identified.

Section.name: Azure DNS
Section.Content: Azure DNS is a hosting service for DNS domains that provides name resolution by using Microsoft Azure infrastructure. By hosting your domains in Azure, you can manage your DNS records by using the same credentials, APIs, tools, and billing as your other Azure services. Azure DNS consists in a global network of DNS name server allowing Azure to offer a 100% SLA on DNS request resolution. Azure DNS can host private and public domains. In the case of private domains, Azure DNS also provides auto-registration of Azure VMs located in a VNet (auto-registration applies to a single VNet).

Section.name: Azure S2S VPN Connection
Section.Content: An Azure site-to-site VPN connection is a virtual private network (VPN) connection between an on-premises network and an Azure Virtual Network (VNet). This connection allows traffic to flow securely between the on-premises network and the VNet in Azure over the internet.

Section.name: Azure ExpressRoute (optional)
Section.Content: Azure ExpressRoute (ER) provides a dedicated connection between Microsoft cloud (including Azure and O365 services) and a telecommunication provider extending the connectivity to a final point, typically a customer location. An ExpressRoute is an alternative to Internet providing dedicated bandwidth and private connectivity (non encrypted by default). It can be combined with a VPN gateway or another ExpressRoute to provide high-availability.

Section.name: Azure CDN
Section.Content: Azure CDN provides content delivery acceleration to end users thanks to Azure worldwide network and by leveraging the closest POP to serve user requests. Azure CDN accelerates both static and dynamic content delivery. Static content can be cached and be subsequently served locally from the closest PoP (offloading also central back-end resources). Dynamic content delivery can also be accelerated by leveraging Microsoft backbone from the closest PoP to the backend resource (instead of the Internet backbone) and by leveraging smart routing optimizations.

Section.name: Azure Load Balancer
Section.Content: An Azure Load Balancer improves application performance and high-availability by distributing client requests between a set of equivalent servers and by continuously checking their health. An Azure Load Balancer helps applications to scale horizontally and remain available in case of local failure or outage impacting an entire availability zone. Moreover, Load Balancer belongs to the rich suite of Azure load-balancing solutions and is specially meant for L4 load-balancing in a single region, other solutions are available for single region L7 load-balancing (Application gateway) and for multi-region HTTP(S)/DNS-based load-balancing (respectively Front Door and Traffic Manager).

Section.name: Azure Application Gateway
Section.Content: An Azure Application Gateway provides the same application performance, scalability and high-availability as a Load Balancer but it also provides additional benefits by its ability to process L7 (HTTP/HTTPS) attributes. An application Gateway is able to make optimal routing decisions based on HTTP(S) URI, headers or query strings. It is also able to terminate TLS sessions, providing data encryption in transit. It is also able to make deeper evaluation of the health of backend resources, including their ability to properly answer HTTP or custom probes. Finally an Application Gateway can optionally be used as a WAF or as an ingress for AKS. Moreover, Application Gateway belongs to the rich suite of Azure load-balancing solutions and is specially meant for L7 load-balancing in a single region, other solutions are available for single region L4 load-balancing (Load Balancer) and for multi-region HTTP(S)/DNS-based load-balancing (respectively Front Door and Traffic Manager).

Section.name: Azure Traffic Manager
Section.Content: Azure Traffic Manager is a DNS-based traffic load balancer. This service allows you to distribute traffic to your public facing applications across the global Azure regions. Traffic Manager also provides your public endpoints with high availability and quick responsiveness. Traffic Manager uses DNS to direct the client requests to the appropriate service endpoint based on a traffic-routing method. Traffic manager also provides health monitoring for every endpoint. The endpoint can be any Internet-facing service hosted inside or outside of Azure. Traffic Manager provides a range of traffic-routing methods and endpoint monitoring options to suit different application needs and automatic failover models. Traffic Manager is resilient to failure, including the failure of an entire Azure region.

Section.name: Azure Firewall
Section.Content: Azure Firewall complements the network filtering function provided by Network Security Groups (NSGs) by adding a few additional benefits including: central management, fully stateful firewall, NAT, FQDN filtering, Threat-intelligence filtering (based on malicious IP and domains). Compared to third-party solutions, it also provides native high-availability and unrestricted scalability.

Section.name: Azure Service Bus
Section.Content: Service Bus provides message brokering services allowing application components to be decoupled and still communicate efficiently and whatever their location, in Azure or on-premises. It supports modern application architecture by enabling asynchronous messaging, transaction processing (with ordered delivery) and consumer load-balancing. Cegeka can help developers to adopt this kind of patterns by offloading them from non application-level activities, allowing them to focus on the business and application logic.

Section.name: Azure Analysis service
Section.Content: Built on SQL Server Analysis Service (SSAS), Azure Analysis Service is a serverless and cloud native data modelling service to analyse and combine data from multiple sources.

Section.name: Azure Databricks
Section.Content: Azure Databricks is a cloud native deployment of Spark based analytics

Section.name: Azure Data factory
Section.Content: Built from SQL SSIS, Azure Data Factory is a cloud native service to build & run data integration pipelines, including ETL or ELT processing. This service allows raw big data from multiple sources to be cleaned and centralised for further processing or analysis.

Section.name: Azure Data lake storage
Section.Content: Data Lake Storage builds on blob storage and extends its native features and benefits (cheap, massively scalable and durable storage) to big data analytics use cases thanks to additional features like file system semantics and file-level security.

Section.name: Azure Machine Learning
Section.Content: Azure machine learning allows a tenant to create a model in Azure Machine Learning or use an open-source model and run them in Azure.

Section.name: Azure Purview
Section.Content: Azure Purview provides a holistic overview and governance service of a tenant data landscape, whatever the location of the data, including Azure, other clouds, on-premises and SaaS data. Governance services include among other data discovery, classification and lineage in general.

Section.name: Azure Synapse Analytics
Section.Content: Formerly known as SQL data warehouse, Azure Synapse Analytics provides a complete big data analysis and integration platform. This includes data warehouse storage (based on Azure Data Lake), SQL & T-SQL distributed query service (including support for streaming data and machine learning models), Spark big data engine, ETL/ELT integration pipelines (similar to Azure data factory pipelines)

Section.name: API management
Section.Content: API management provides a central point to expose APIs securely and at scale. It allows application owner to define API consumption policies like quota & throttling. It also simplifies API consumption by means of a developer portal for consumer onboarding (API documentation, interactive console, account creation, quota visibility,…) & productization. Finally it also provides mechanisms to control their lifecycle, including updates & visibility. Cegeka can help developers to adopt this kind of patterns by offloading them from non application-level activities, allowing them to focus on the API logic.

Section.name: Remote workplace Services (Citrix)
Section.Content: The Remote workplace services is the service that manages the Central workspace infrastructure, a virtual workspace located at Cegeka’s datacenters and remotely available for end-users. This service manages the following topics:

Section.name: Local Workplace Services (Image & OS Build Maintenance)
Section.Header: Local Workplace Services (Image & OS Build Maintenance)
